Child Rights Advocacy and research director for Save the Children. Save the Children welcomes opportunity to provide an oral submission on the education and training Early childhood Education Reform Amendment Bill Save the Children. As one of the world's leading child rights organizations founded more than 100 years ago, to uphold and advance the rights of children here in New Zealand and globally.

And we have been working here in New Zealand for more than 75 years. Save the Children has a vested interest in the education system as it plays a critical role in ensuring the social, emotional and educational development of children their wellbeing. And in upholding their rights. The New Zealand government is obligated to uphold the rights of children under the convention on the rights of the child.

And the early childhood system directly impacts their rights. Quality. Early childhood education can be transformational for children, their parents and society. And therefore it's critical that any changes to regulations and or legislation will not negatively impact the quality of early childhood education.

Save the Children does not support the proposed amendments in the education and training Amendment Bill, and recommends that the bill should not proceed in its current form. In our submission, we wish to make four recommendations. One, take a child rights approach to ensure that children's rights under the convention on the rights of the child, understood, respected and upheld across the early childhood education system, undertake a child impact assessment before any proposed changes implemented, including changes to legislation.

Three, retain the current purpose of the early childhood education of early childhood education, stated in part two of the Education and Training Act 2020 and four. Do not establish a director of regulation that will have a significant impact on the provision, quality and shape of early childhood education that sits outside of the Ministry of Education.

We strongly recommend a child rights approach to ensure the three pillars of children's rights protection, provision, and participation are understood, respected, and upheld throughout the early childhood education system of our New Zealand. Additionally, that ECE system should respect and uphold the rights of TAM and their whanau.

Under Ang any proposed changes should to the ACT should be required to undertake a child impact assessment that thoroughly assesses the impact of proposed changes on children, their families, and the teaching profession. It should also consider whether any changes would support the profession and the quality of care and education provision.

The current purpose of quality, sorry. It is a recommendation of Save the Children. That this, uh, the current purpose stated under part two of the act is fit for purpose and should remain the current purpose on quality EC provision and learning children's health, safety and wellbeing. While making provision for parental choice is fit for purpose, the ability for parents to participate in the labor market is a secondary outcome and is directly enabled by the quality provision of the initial purpose, quality, accessible, and affordable.

Early childhood education should be first driven by children's care and education needs and support families in the care and education of their young children. It should not be primarily driven by parental engagement in the paid workforce. Parents already struggle with the need to put their young children into care due to pressures of the paid workforce.

And this could be exacerbated of workforce participation is seen as the primary purpose of the early childhood education system. Prior research has shown that New Zealand parents already engage in long working hours and are among the highest in the world. Previous census data has shown that these working, the those working the longest hours have no qualifications and are on lower incomes.

Additionally, previous previous research by the former Families Commission found that parents and long workouts had negative impacts on family wellbeing. Existing high rates of parents and paid work demonstrates that engagement and paid workforce is already taking place and therefore is not necessary to include as the primary purpose of the early childhood education system.

We are concerned that the proposed director of regulation, a role that will have significant influence and qu on the quality and shape of early childhood education provision, will sit outside the Ministry of Education. The director of regulation appears to be focused on EC regulations related to the operation of centers and is not clear how the quality and provision of education and care outcomes for children are considered or applied in relation to this role.

Quality education care outcomes for children, including their health, safety, wellbeing, and education, are completely reliant on centers or an alternative model ability to deliver education, quality education provision. This delivery is directly related to and impacted by regulations. Therefore, it is concerning that the proposal that the proposed model seems to imply a separation between quality education provision and operational regulations.

It is our view that these roles should be integrated. And we are concerned that a partial implementation of the EC regulations review may lead to an extensive period of change and therefore disruption. It is our view that all necessary changes should be proposed and thoroughly consulted on in a coherent way before being agreed and then enforced through legislative changes.

The review found the EC system was outdated and not up to standard of other regulatory systems in New Zealand and, and incomparable countries. And the current proposal, it is difficult to see how amendments to the Pro Proposed Education Training Act will achieve that improvement of provision and delivery.

Changes to legislation should be be carefully considered and based on clear evidence and to ensure the intended outcomes are achieved and thereby avoiding unintended negative consequences. Thank you.
